sourceEvidence.label: AetherLink development-relay source snapshot supporting a production security design review

assessment.summary: The development relay now has tactical source-aware allocation controls, source peer quotas, bounded waiting, and post-proof authenticated identity fairness, while authenticated signed allocation leases and monotonic pair-epoch recovery with immediate deny-only revocation remain selection-gated production hardening opportunities.

nonNegotiables: Android remains a client of the trusted runtime and never calls model backends directly.
Relay and allocation infrastructure never receives endpoint traffic secrets or decrypts AI frames.
QR remains the out-of-band device trust and recovery ceremony.
Production routes never downgrade automatically to development plaintext protocols.
assumptions: No measured public-network latency or memory budget was supplied.
P2P NAT traversal and physical-device proof are separate roadmap milestones.
One-sided emergency denial is safer than unilateral trust replacement.

opportunities.title: Authenticate allocation and bind service-issued leases
opportunities.summary: Protect allocation credentials with TLS and make leases independently verifiable without exposing endpoint traffic secrets.
opportunities.diagnosis: Runtime and paired-device proofs authenticate endpoints to a plain TCP allocation service, but the service has no authenticated signed lease identity toward endpoints.

evidence.claim: Allocation uses a TCP socket and accepts a shape-validated response with no service signature.
Endpoint traffic keys derive from endpoint ECDH plus a runtime-local relay secret that is not sent to the allocation service.
Endpoint signatures prevent unauthorized allocation ownership but do not authenticate the service or conceal bearer allocation credentials.

Allocation-prefixed attempts consume separate bounded accepted-source buckets before full parsing; exact strict preflight classification, shared overflow, and full-refill-before-idle validation prevent looser-bucket and reset bypasses, while source-free metrics preserve telemetry privacy. This development control does not authenticate the service.

Unmatched rooms have a first-insertion monotonic deadline capped by lease expiry, registration or readiness decisions atomically expire late rooms before matching or replacement, and waiting results carry the deadline without a post-publication room lookup. Verified runtime or paired-client identities have role-separated cross-source waiting quotas. Bootstrap and legacy peers remain source-only. This development control does not authenticate the service or sign leases.

desiredInvariants: Every production allocation request authenticates the intended service before disclosing allocation credentials or route tokens.
Every accepted lease is signed over exact service, endpoint ownership, pair epoch, generation, nonce, expiry, suite, and authorization digest fields.
The signed lease digest and service key id are bound into endpoint registration, key confirmation, and device authentication transcripts.

Paired endpoints directly verify each other's signatures over both identities, both ephemeral shares, both session nonces, pair epoch, generation, and signed lease digest before installing traffic keys.
The relay never receives endpoint traffic secrets or plaintext AI protocol payloads.
Service keyset versions and production protocol floors cannot roll back.

options.title: Signed TCP inside an authenticated private overlay
options.summary: Add a pinned service signing root and signed lease while an authenticated private overlay supplies channel identity and secrecy.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Signatures authenticate control messages, but bearer and route material remain observable without an authenticated private overlay.
Endpoint-owned encryption remains necessary and unchanged.
The implemented development-relay limiter remains defense in depth but does not satisfy service authentication or lease integrity.
The bounded-waiting and authenticated-identity fairness control remains defense in depth and does not satisfy service authentication or signed lease integrity.

tradeoffs.assessment: Forged challenges and leases fail signature verification, while credential secrecy depends on correct overlay enrollment, firewall, and routing policy.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Inject forged and replayed service messages, remove overlay policy, and prove production routes fail closed before exposing bearer material.

tradeoffs.assessment: One bounded signature verification is added without another network handshake.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Benchmark allocation latency and CPU before and after service signature verification.

tradeoffs.assessment: Only a bounded keyset and lease object are retained.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Compare peak RSS during repeated allocation and keyset rotation tests.

tradeoffs.assessment: Topology remains simple, but signing-key, clock, overlay routing, or firewall failures can reject or accidentally expose allocations.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Inject unavailable signer, expired key, skewed clock, stale keyset, and missing-overlay failures.

tradeoffs.assessment: A service root, online key, rotation procedure, and signature telemetry become required.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run normal and emergency key rotation drills with overlap and rollback attempts.

tradeoffs.assessment: The current line protocol can carry signatures with a narrow versioned extension.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run dual-version compatibility tests and prove production routes reject unsigned fallback.

residualRisks: Overlay enrollment, firewall, or routing mistakes can expose the plain application channel.
A compromised online signer can mint leases during its validity window.

workPackages: Define canonical signed challenge, lease, and keyset objects.
acceptanceCriteria: Unsigned, wrong-key, expired, and lower-keyset service messages fail closed.
migrationNotes: Use only inside an authenticated private overlay if production credentials remain on plain TCP.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Disable the production route class; never silently accept unsigned messages for an enrolled route.

options.title: TLS 1.3 plus signed lease capabilities
options.summary: Protect allocation with TLS and bind a delegated service-signed lease into endpoint transcripts.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: TLS protects credentials and authenticates the channel; the signed lease authenticates state after the channel closes.
Endpoint-owned PSK-mixed ECDH remains the data-plane confidentiality control.
The shared protocol gains service key, signed lease, epoch, and digest fields.
The implemented development-relay limiter remains defense in depth but does not satisfy service authentication or lease integrity.
The bounded-waiting and authenticated-identity fairness control remains defense in depth and does not satisfy service authentication or signed lease integrity.

tradeoffs.assessment: Network observers cannot read allocation credentials, and endpoints independently verify lease integrity and service key epoch.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run MITM, pin mismatch, forged lease, stolen-token-on-another-channel, and downgrade tests.

tradeoffs.assessment: Cold allocation gains a TLS handshake and signature verification; resumption can reduce repeated cost.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure cold and resumed p50/p95/p99 allocation latency over public and private-overlay RTTs.

tradeoffs.assessment: TLS state, trust roots, cached keysets, and lease objects add bounded memory.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure client, runtime, and service peak RSS under concurrent allocation load.

tradeoffs.assessment: Standard TLS removes custom channel-authentication risk, while certificate, clock, and keyset failures become fail-closed dependencies.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Test expiry, skew, rotation overlap, trust-store failure, keyset cache, and service failover.

tradeoffs.assessment: Production needs root custody, delegated key rotation, certificate operations, and downgrade telemetry.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Complete scheduled and emergency rotation drills and verify alert coverage.

tradeoffs.assessment: Allocation transport, schema, endpoint transcripts, and route classes change across Swift and Kotlin.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Use separate v2 development and v3 production routes with fixed cross-language vectors and no automatic fallback.

residualRisks: The allocation service still observes allocation metadata and source IPs.
A compromised online signer can mint leases until revoked.
Endpoint compromise and service denial remain possible.

workPackages: Define service keysets and signed lease capability.
Add TLS allocation transport and explicit route trust source.
Implement a reviewed peer-verifiable endpoint identity KEX state machine.
Bind lease digest, identities, ephemeral shares, nonces, pair epoch, and generation into endpoint transcripts.
Add MITM, rotation, blindness, and performance tests.
acceptanceCriteria: Production allocation discloses credentials only after service authentication.
Both endpoints reject mutated, expired, revoked, wrong-service, and lower-keyset leases.
Both endpoints reject peer identity or ephemeral substitution even when the relay admits and forwards the altered transcript.
Relay captures contain no endpoint traffic secret or AI plaintext.
migrationNotes: Run v2 development and v3 production on distinct route classes or ports.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Disable v3 route issuance while preserving the highest accepted protocol floor on enrolled endpoints.

options.title: Split allocation authority and blind relay
options.summary: Separate lease minting from ciphertext forwarding and use portable opaque capabilities.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: The authenticated authority owns allocation, while relay nodes cannot mint accepted leases.
Endpoint-owned data-plane encryption remains mandatory.
Pair co-authorization moves into a portable capability but still requires endpoint proofs.
The implemented development-relay limiter remains defense in depth but does not satisfy authority isolation or lease integrity.
The bounded-waiting and authenticated-identity fairness control remains defense in depth and does not satisfy authority isolation or signed lease integrity.

tradeoffs.assessment: Relay compromise cannot mint leases, and relay nodes receive less allocation identity metadata.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Compromise a relay test node and prove it cannot mint, alter, or redeem unauthorized capabilities.

tradeoffs.assessment: Capability redemption or cache validation adds a hop or cache lookup to admission.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Benchmark offline verification and online redemption at target concurrency and RTT.

tradeoffs.assessment: Authority, relay key caches, revocation state, and redemption caches add bounded but distributed state.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure cache and revocation memory growth by active lease count and region.

tradeoffs.assessment: Compartmentalization improves blast radius but introduces authority/cache partition behavior.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run authority outage, stale cache, region partition, replay, and failover drills.

tradeoffs.assessment: A second service, key distribution, revocation fanout, and multi-region incidents require ownership.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Define SLOs, dashboards, on-call runbooks, and rotation/revocation drills before rollout.

tradeoffs.assessment: Deployment topology and capability admission change while endpoints must remain compatible.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: First deploy Option 2's portable lease, then move verification to split relay nodes behind the same endpoint format.

residualRisks: The authority still observes allocation metadata.
Relay nodes still observe traffic timing, volume, roles, and opaque pair identifiers.
Authority/relay partition policy can create availability or stale-revocation windows.

workPackages: Separate authority and relay admission around a portable signed capability.
acceptanceCriteria: A compromised relay node cannot mint or extend a lease and can forward only authorized ciphertext rooms.
migrationNotes: Adopt only after the signed capability format is stable under Option 2.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Route capability verification back through the combined service without changing endpoint lease verification.

opportunities.recommendation: Use TLS 1.3 plus a delegated service-signed lease now, while keeping the capability portable enough for a later authority/relay split.

opportunities.title: Add pair epoch recovery and immediate revocation
opportunities.summary: Make compromise recovery a monotonic state machine that can deny access immediately but requires fresh QR authority for key replacement.
opportunities.diagnosis: Lease generations are durable and pair-authorized, but no higher pair epoch or signed revocation counter invalidates old keys, backups, leases, and active rooms together.

evidence.claim: The allocation registry enforces generation CAS and consumed-bootstrap tombstones without a pair epoch or revocation counter.
Android persists pinned runtime and route lease state but no monotonic recovery receipt.
The protocol cannot express a signed recovery or immediate global revocation transition.

Paired claim and renewal attempts, including malformed renewal records, consume the development relay allocation-mutation source bucket before full parsing, and idle deletion cannot reset capacity before full refill. This adds no pair epoch or revocation authority.

Unmatched rooms have lease-capped first-insertion deadlines, registration or readiness decisions atomically expire late rooms before matching or replacement, and waiting results carry the deadline without a post-publication room lookup. Verified runtime or paired-client identities have role-separated cross-source waiting quotas after proof. Bootstrap and legacy peers remain source-only. This does not add a pair epoch, recovery authority, or revocation transition.

desiredInvariants: Every lease and session binds a monotonic pair epoch and revocation counter.
Normal renewal is dual-signed by current endpoint keys.
Either current endpoint may revoke access but may not authorize a replacement key alone.
Key replacement requires fresh QR authority, fresh endpoint secret material, and pair epoch plus one.
Relays close active and waiting rooms for revoked state and reject lower or replayed state.
Offline endpoints require a current signed state receipt before reactivation.

Every mutation is idempotent by transition id and canonical request digest: the same id and digest returns the original signed receipt, the same id with different content fails, and a competing id returns the signed winning state. A read-only authenticated status operation returns that state without changing authority state.

options.title: Short leases and manual re-pair
options.summary: Reduce exposure using short dual-signed leases and explicit local delete/fresh QR operations.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Shorter generation leases narrow replay duration but do not invalidate an old long-term key family.
No recovery or global revocation protocol is added.
The implemented development-relay limiter remains defense in depth but does not create recovery state or immediate revocation.
The bounded-waiting and authenticated-identity fairness control remains defense in depth and does not create recovery state or immediate revocation.

tradeoffs.assessment: A stolen lease expires sooner, but a compromised long-term key or active room remains outside immediate revocation.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure usable replay windows for stolen leases and active sessions.

tradeoffs.assessment: Shorter leases increase dual-signature renewal frequency and control-plane traffic.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Load-test renewal rate and endpoint battery/CPU cost across lease durations.

tradeoffs.assessment: Existing lease state is reused without new long-lived records.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Confirm allocation-store size remains proportional to current leases and tombstones.

tradeoffs.assessment: Authority outages become user-visible sooner because leases expire more often.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run outage tests across candidate lease durations and measure disconnect rates.

tradeoffs.assessment: Support must guide manual delete and fresh QR re-pair without a signed recovery receipt.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run recovery UX tests and incident drills for lost client and rotated runtime.

tradeoffs.assessment: Lease duration and UX can change without a new protocol state machine.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Verify current clients handle the selected lease duration and explicit re-pair path.

residualRisks: Old backups have no pair-epoch rollback signal.
Compromised long-term keys can remain useful.

workPackages: Tune lease policy and harden explicit delete/re-pair UX.
acceptanceCriteria: Expired leases and locally removed trust cannot reconnect.

options.title: Monotonic pair epoch state machine
options.summary: Bind leases and sessions to pair epoch, allow deny-only emergency revocation, and require fresh QR for replacement.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: Dual-signed renewal becomes the normal same-epoch transition.
Fresh QR becomes the explicit authority for key replacement and epoch advancement.
Generation is nested under a durable pair epoch and revocation counter.
The protocol gains recovery, revocation, and state receipt objects.
The implemented development-relay limiter remains defense in depth but does not create pair epoch, revocation, or signed status state.
The bounded-waiting and authenticated-identity fairness control remains defense in depth and does not create pair epoch, revocation, or signed status state.

tradeoffs.assessment: Old keys, leases, sessions, and backups fail after a higher signed epoch/counter; one key can deny but cannot replace trust.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Model every transition and replay old artifacts after epoch and counter advancement.

tradeoffs.assessment: State receipts, extra signature checks, and revocation events add small control-plane work outside frame forwarding.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure transition throughput, reconnect latency, and revocation fanout under load.

tradeoffs.assessment: Endpoints and authority retain bounded current pair state, receipt, transition id, and tombstones.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure storage growth by pair count, epoch transitions, and tombstone retention policy.

tradeoffs.assessment: Idempotent signed state supports deterministic crash recovery, but fanout and persistence failures can lock out routes.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Inject crashes and response loss before and after authority commit, then require signed read-only status convergence without a second mutation.

tradeoffs.assessment: Operations gain revocation monitoring, recovery diagnostics, state inspection, and user lockout support.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run lost-client, rotated-runtime, authority-outage, and delayed-fanout incident drills.

tradeoffs.assessment: Schema, endpoint storage, allocation state, relay matcher, key exchange, auth, and QR recovery all gain pair state.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Deploy parse-only receipts, observe comparisons, then enforce only on production-v3 routes.

residualRisks: A compromised endpoint can issue denial-only revocation.
Both endpoint keys compromised before recovery defeat pair authorization.
Secure-storage rollback may require online receipt refresh and user re-pair.

workPackages: Define canonical pair state, transitions, receipts, and read-only status lookup.
Persist highest accepted state crash-safely on all owners.
Add deny-only revocation and active-room closure.
Add fresh QR replacement ceremonies and secret rotation.
acceptanceCriteria: Lower or replayed pair state fails everywhere even with a valid old signature.
A response lost after authority commit converges through signed status lookup without advancing state twice.
Emergency revocation closes active and waiting rooms within the selected SLO.
Replacement requires fresh QR and rotates endpoint traffic material.
migrationNotes: Observe signed state before enforcing on production-v3 routes.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Stop issuing new transitions, but never lower an endpoint's highest accepted pair state.

options.title: Threshold recovery
options.summary: Use two-of-N devices or an offline recovery key to authorize pair-epoch replacement.

evidenceCoverage.rationale: QR pairing remains enrollment input, while a quorum can later authorize replacement.
A new recovery membership and threshold transition protocol is required.
The implemented development-relay limiter remains defense in depth but does not create threshold recovery authority.
The bounded-waiting and authenticated-identity fairness control remains defense in depth and does not create threshold recovery authority.

tradeoffs.assessment: One lost or compromised key cannot authorize replacement if the configured quorum remains honest.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Model minority compromise, stale shares, conflicting quorums, and offline-key theft.

tradeoffs.assessment: Recovery collects and verifies multiple signatures but does not affect normal frame forwarding.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure recovery latency and signature cost across candidate quorum sizes.

tradeoffs.assessment: Membership, share metadata, receipts, and audit state grow with trusted recovery members.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Measure endpoint and authority state by member count and transition history.

tradeoffs.assessment: Quorum tolerates one lost key but introduces partition and member-availability failure modes.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Test member loss, partition, conflicting recovery attempts, and stale offline shares.

tradeoffs.assessment: Recovery membership, offline key custody, share revocation, and audit become product operations.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Run enrollment, backup, lost-share, compromised-member, and quorum-change drills.

tradeoffs.assessment: The one-runtime/one-client model becomes a multi-member recovery authority plane.
tradeoffs.validationPlan: Deploy only after pair epoch is stable and multi-client recovery requirements are approved.

residualRisks: Recovery membership can become an account-like authority.
Offline recovery key theft can defeat the intended quorum.
Partitions and stale shares can cause lockout or conflicting recovery attempts.

workPackages: Define recovery membership and threshold policy after a product decision.
acceptanceCriteria: No minority below the configured quorum can replace a pair key.
implementationReadiness.rollback: Stop new quorum enrollment while retaining already accepted epoch history.

opportunities.recommendation: Use monotonic pair epoch with dual-signed normal renewal, one-sided deny-only revocation, and fresh QR replacement; defer threshold recovery.

openQuestions: Which service trust root provisions public, enterprise, and private-overlay routes?
Is one-sided denial-of-service an acceptable emergency revocation authority?
What lease freshness and revocation-to-room-close SLOs are required?
How should Android and macOS detect local secure-storage rollback after OS backup restore?
When should allocation authority and relay data plane become separate deployments?
